ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Raita. Nearest parent is a yogurt-based condiment with vegetables (FOODON:03315763 — yogurt-based food product). A new concept for Indian raita preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Yogurt and cucumber condiment, spiced (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for raita)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for raita; A12AC (fermented milk products / yogurt-based) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for raita as a prepared food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raita is one small bowl of approximately 150 g, served as a cooling condiment alongside biryani, paratha, or spicy curries.

nutrient_source.title: Raita — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F01040003; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore (Foodo Database); Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo Food Code F01040003 (Raita), cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for validation of protein, calcium, and micronutrient values. Both are approved primary sources for Indian dishes served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Yogurt-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate from plain whole-milk yogurt with cucumber
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for raita as a composite dish. GI of 35 is estimated from plain whole-milk yogurt (GI ~36, University of Sydney GI Database) with cucumber (GI negligible). The yogurt protein and fat matrix substantially blunts glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 6.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.4 g. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 225 mg per 100 g, primarily from added salt and chaat masala
hypertension.guidance: Raita contributes approximately 225 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or request a low-salt preparation, while benefiting from the calcium (102 mg/100 g) and potassium (165 mg/100 g) content which support blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic index (~35) and low glycaemic load (~3.4 per 150 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raita is an excellent condiment choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes — its GI of approximately 35 and glycaemic load of ~3.4 per standard serving are negligible, and the yogurt protein-fat matrix can moderate the overall glycaemic response of the accompanying meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g), zero trans fat, and beneficial calcium and potassium profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raita has a favourable cardiovascular profile with low saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g), confirmed zero trans fat, and meaningful potassium (165 mg/100 g) and calcium (102 mg/100 g) content; sodium at 225 mg/100 g should be monitored in patients on sodium-restricted cardiac diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic yogurt base and anti-inflammatory roasted cumin
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The probiotic content of fresh yogurt supports gut microbiome health relevant to systemic inflammation; roasted cumin has documented anti-inflammatory properties, and cucumber is very low in purines, making raita a suitable condiment for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raita is the quintessential cooling Indian condiment made from plain whole-milk yogurt (dahi) whisked smooth and combined with diced fresh cucumber, roasted cumin powder, a pinch of red chilli powder, chaat masala, salt, and fresh coriander leaves, served cold. It is a no-cook, minimally processed preparation that takes minutes to assemble and is ubiquitous across North and South Indian cuisine. The texture is creamy and slightly thick from the yogurt, with refreshing crunch from the cucumber and aromatic warmth from the cumin. Raita serves as the essential palate-cooling counterbalance to spicy biryanis, parathas, aloo paratha, and rich curries. Nutritionally, raita is one of the lowest-calorie and lowest-GI condiments in Indian cuisine at approximately 62 kcal per 100 g, with a GI of ~35 and a glycaemic load of ~3.4 per 150 g serving. It provides meaningful calcium (102 mg/100 g) and potassium (165 mg/100 g) from the yogurt base, making it beneficial for bone health and blood pressure. The saturated fat content is low at 1.8 g/100 g and trans fat is zero. Sodium at 225 mg/100 g is moderate and comes primarily from added salt and chaat masala. Raita is suitable for vegetarians, halal diets, and gluten-free diets. It is widely available at Indian restaurants, North Indian restaurants, and as a standard accompaniment to biryani across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo Food Code F01040003 (exact match for Raita as served in Singapore), cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. Both are approved primary sources. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (plain yogurt GI ~36) with documented estimation basis, confidence 0.9.
notes: trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — this is consistent with a minimally processed yogurt-based condiment containing no hydrogenated fats. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per source — plain whole-milk yogurt contains negligible vitamin D unless fortified, and this preparation is unfortified. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are -1.0 because the source does not report these specific values. water_g is -1.0 because the source does not report this specific value. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database using plain whole-milk yogurt as the closest reference food; no direct GI measurement for raita as a composite dish was found in the database. Glycaemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (35 × 6.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.4. Portions: HPB Foodo lists 100 g as standard; GrabFood platform lists 150 g small bowl — 150 g used as default serving for GL calculation as it reflects real-world restaurant portion. Two is_default:true entries in input portions resolved by selecting 150 g (GrabFood) as the serving size for GL and 100 g as the per_100g reference basis.
